questions.text: The 'Vernacular Press Act' of 1878, which controlled Indian-language newspapers, was introduced by which Viceroy?
options: Lord Ripon
Lord Lytton
Lord Curzon
Lord Dufferin
questions.explanation: The Vernacular Press Act (1878) was introduced by Lord Lytton. Known as the 'Gagging Act', it allowed the government to seize printing presses of Indian-language newspapers that published content deemed seditious. The Act applied only to vernacular (Indian-language) press — English newspapers were exempt, which made it doubly resented as both repressive and racially discriminatory. It was repealed by Lord Ripon in 1882, his very first major act as Viceroy.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Vernacular Press Act 1878 and Lord Lytton is one of the most frequently tested Viceroy-Act pairings

questions.text: The partition of Bengal in 1905 was carried out by which Viceroy, and in which year was it reversed?
options: Lord Curzon in 1905; reversed in 1911 by Lord Hardinge II
Lord Minto in 1905; reversed in 1911 by Lord Chelmsford
Lord Curzon in 1903; reversed in 1908 by Lord Minto II
Lord Dufferin in 1905; reversed in 1915 by Lord Hardinge II
questions.explanation: The Partition of Bengal was carried out by Lord Curzon on 16 October 1905, dividing Bengal into East Bengal and Assam (Muslim majority, capital Dacca) and West Bengal (Hindu majority, capital Calcutta). The partition triggered the Swadeshi Movement — the most intense anti-British agitation of the era. It was reversed in 1911 by Lord Hardinge II at the Delhi Durbar, when King George V visited India. Bengal was reunified but a new partition was made: Punjab and Delhi were separated, with Delhi becoming the new capital of British India.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Curzon-Partition of Bengal (1905) and Hardinge II reversal (1911) is one of the most standard Viceroy PYQ pairings

questions.text: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre (13 April 1919) occurred during the tenure of which Viceroy, and who ordered the firing?
options: Lord Chelmsford; General Dyer
Lord Irwin; General Dyer
Lord Reading; Colonel O'Dwyer
Lord Chelmsford; Michael O'Dwyer
questions.explanation: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre took place on 13 April 1919 (Baisakhi Day) during Lord Chelmsford's tenure as Viceroy. General Reginald Dyer ordered troops to open fire on a crowd gathered in the enclosed garden at Amritsar, Punjab, without warning. Official figures put the dead at 379; Indian National Congress estimated nearly 1,000. Michael O'Dwyer was the Lt. Governor of Punjab who supported Dyer's action (he was later assassinated in London by Udham Singh in 1940). The massacre permanently changed the nature of India's freedom struggle — Tagore returned his knighthood in protest.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Jallianwala Bagh 1919, Chelmsford, Dyer is one of the most frequently tested massacre-Viceroy facts

questions.text: The Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931) resulted in which key decision regarding the Civil Disobedience Movement?
options: Gandhi agreed to permanently abandon civil disobedience and accept Dominion Status
Gandhi agreed to suspend the Civil Disobedience Movement and attend the Second Round Table Conference; the British agreed to release political prisoners
The British agreed to grant India complete independence within one year
Gandhi agreed to join the Viceroy's Executive Council as a member
questions.explanation: The Gandhi-Irwin Pact (5 March 1931) was a negotiated agreement: Gandhi agreed to suspend the Civil Disobedience Movement and attend the Second Round Table Conference in London. The British agreed to: release political prisoners who had not committed acts of violence; restore confiscated properties of protesters; allow coastal villagers to make salt; permit Congress to picket foreign cloth shops. The Pact was significant as the British negotiated directly with Gandhi as an equal — the first time Congress was formally acknowledged as a representative body. The Second RTC ended inconclusively; the movement resumed in 1932.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Gandhi-Irwin Pact 1931, terms, and significance is a frequently tested topic

questions.text: Lord Canning was both the last Governor-General of India and the first Viceroy of India. Which Act created the Viceroy's office?
options: Government of India Act 1858
Indian Councils Act 1861
Pitt's India Act 1784
Charter Act 1853
questions.explanation: The Government of India Act 1858 (also called the Act for the Better Government of India) abolished the East India Company's administration and transferred power to the British Crown. It created the office of Viceroy — the Crown's representative in India — replacing the Governor-General's earlier relationship with the Company. Lord Canning, who was Governor-General at the time of the 1857 Revolt, became the first Viceroy under this new arrangement. The Act also created the Secretary of State for India (a Cabinet position in London) with a Council of India to advise them.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because students often confuse which specific Act created the Viceroy's office; the Government of India Act 1858 is the correct answer

questions.text: The Ilbert Bill controversy of 1883 is considered a turning point in Indian nationalism. Which of the following correctly describes what happened?
options: Lord Ilbert proposed Indian newspapers could criticize British officials; European planters organized against it and the bill was withdrawn
The Ilbert Bill proposed Indian judges could try European British subjects; European community's organized opposition forced the bill to be diluted; Indians learned that organized agitation works
Ilbert proposed Indian lawyers could appear in British courts; the bill passed unchanged and gave Indians equal legal rights
The bill proposed reservations for Indians in ICS examinations; it was withdrawn after British protests
questions.explanation: The Ilbert Bill (1883) was proposed by Law Member C.P. Ilbert during Lord Ripon's tenure. It aimed to remove the racial exemption that protected Europeans from being tried by Indian judges and magistrates. The European community — planters, traders, officials — mounted a massive organized campaign against it, fearing 'trial by natives.' Under pressure, the bill was diluted: a European could demand a jury half of whom were European. However, the real significance was the lesson it taught Indians: the European community had organized politically and forced a British policy reversal. This showed that organized political agitation could change policy — directly inspiring the formation of the Indian National Congress in 1885.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the Ilbert Bill's significance (lesson for nationalism) is a nuanced point tested in analytical questions

questions.text: The Morley-Minto Reforms (1909) introduced 'separate electorates' for Muslims. What was the immediate demand that led to this?
options: The Muslim League passed a resolution demanding separate electorates at its 1906 Dacca session
A delegation led by the Aga Khan visited Viceroy Minto in 1906 to request guaranteed Muslim representation, separate from the general electorate
Gandhi demanded separate electorates for Muslims as part of the Congress-League unity pact
The British census of 1901 recommended separate representation as the only practical solution
questions.explanation: In October 1906, a delegation of 35 Muslim leaders led by the Aga Khan III met Viceroy Minto at Simla. They argued that given the overall Hindu majority in India, Muslims would always be underrepresented in any general electorate. They requested that seats be reserved for Muslims, to be filled by Muslim-only elections. Minto, sympathetic to the idea of dividing Indian opinion along religious lines, agreed in principle. This understanding led directly to the separate electorate provisions in the Morley-Minto Reforms (Indian Councils Act 1909). It also led to the founding of the All-India Muslim League in December 1906 at Dacca.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the specific mechanism (Aga Khan delegation to Minto, 1906) leading to separate electorates is a multi-step causal question tested in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: Lord Ripon is called the 'Father of Local Self-Government in India.' Which resolution established elected local bodies?
options: The Decentralization Resolution of 1870 under Lord Mayo
The Local Self-Government Resolution of 1882 under Lord Ripon
The Municipalities Act of 1884 under Lord Dufferin
The Hunter Commission Report of 1882 recommended elected local bodies
questions.explanation: The Local Self-Government Resolution of 1882, issued by Lord Ripon, established elected local bodies (municipalities and district boards) across India. For the first time, Indians would be elected to these bodies and given limited control over local affairs — sanitation, roads, schools, public health. Lord Mayo had begun financial decentralization in 1870 (transferring some revenue responsibilities to provinces), but elected local bodies were Ripon's innovation. Ripon's resolution is the historical precursor to the 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendments (1992) that constitutionally mandated panchayati raj and urban local bodies.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the Local Self-Government Resolution (1882) is covered in basics but the specific year and connection to 73rd/74th Amendments is a nuanced test point

questions.text: Lord Mayo (1869-1872) holds a tragic distinction among Viceroys. What was it?
options: He was the first Viceroy to die in office of natural causes (heart failure at Simla)
He was the first Viceroy to be assassinated — stabbed by a convict at the Andaman Islands
He resigned in disgrace after the Bengal Famine of 1870
He was dismissed by the Secretary of State for publicly criticizing Crown policy
questions.explanation: Lord Mayo was the first (and only) Viceroy of India to be assassinated. On 8 February 1872, during an official visit to the Port Blair penal settlement in the Andaman Islands, he was stabbed by Sher Ali, an Afghan convict who had been serving a life sentence for murder. Mayo was a reforming Viceroy who began financial decentralization and established the Mayo College at Ajmer (for Indian princes). Despite his short tenure, his assassination was significant enough that the Andaman Islands later became associated with Indian political prisoners — the Cellular Jail ('Kala Pani') became a symbol of colonial repression.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Lord Mayo is a 'minor Viceroy' often skipped in notes; his assassination (first ever) is the key distinguishing fact

questions.text: The Montagu Declaration of 1917 is considered a landmark in India's constitutional history. What did it announce?
options: That India would receive complete independence within 25 years
That Indians would be allowed to sit the Indian Civil Service examinations in India, not just in London
That British policy aimed at 'increasing association of Indians in every branch of administration' and eventually 'responsible government'
That the Viceroy's Executive Council would have a majority of Indian members by 1920
questions.explanation: The Montagu Declaration (August 1917), made by Secretary of State Edwin Montagu in the British Parliament, stated that British policy aimed at 'the increasing association of Indians in every branch of the administration and the gradual development of self-governing institutions with a view to the progressive realisation of responsible government in India as an integral part of the British Empire.' This was the first explicit British acknowledgment that self-government was the goal — though the timeline was left deliberately vague. It was made during Lord Chelmsford's tenure and led to the Montagu-Chelmsford Report (1918) and Government of India Act 1919 (Dyarchy). Indian nationalists were disappointed by its vagueness but took it as an implicit commitment.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because students often jump from Jallianwala Bagh (1919) backward without noting the 1917 declaration that preceded it and shaped British-India negotiations

questions.text:
Match the Viceroys with the events associated with them:
1. Lord Curzon   A. Dandi March
2. Lord Irwin    B. Partition of Bengal
3. Lord Chelmsford  C. Jallianwala Bagh
4. Lord Mountbatten  D. Indian Independence Act
questions.explanation: The correct matches: Lord Curzon (1899-1905) — Partition of Bengal (1905); Lord Irwin (1926-1931) — Dandi March/Salt Satyagraha (1930) and Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931); Lord Chelmsford (1916-1921) — Jallianwala Bagh massacre (13 April 1919) and Rowlatt Act (1919); Lord Mountbatten (1947) — Indian Independence Act 1947, Mountbatten Plan (3 June 1947), and Indian Independence (15 August 1947). This match-the-Viceroy pattern is the most tested format in SSC CGL Modern History.
questions.source: Viceroy-Event matching is the single most common question pattern in SSC CGL Modern History Viceroys; tested repeatedly in Tier I and II papers

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describes the significance of the Morley-Minto Reforms (1909)?
options: They granted India Dominion Status and made the Viceroy's Council fully elected
They introduced the concept of Dyarchy in provinces, dividing subjects into transferred and reserved categories
They expanded legislative councils and introduced separate electorates for Muslims — institutionalizing communal representation in Indian politics
They established a fully elected Central Legislature and gave Indians control over law-making
questions.explanation: The Morley-Minto Reforms (Indian Councils Act 1909) had two major features: (1) They expanded the membership and powers of legislative councils at the central and provincial levels — allowing elected members for the first time, though officials still had a majority; (2) They introduced separate electorates for Muslims — Muslims would vote in separate constituencies to elect Muslim members only, guaranteed seats regardless of their proportion of the population. The Dyarchy feature (option B) came later with the Government of India Act 1919 (Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms). The 1909 Reforms are historically significant because separate electorates permanently altered Indian politics — dividing the electorate along religious lines and eventually contributing to Partition.
questions.source: The Morley-Minto Reforms and separate electorates is one of the most frequently tested constitutional acts in SSC CGL Modern History; also tested by asking which reforms introduced separate electorates

questions.text: The Quit India Movement was launched on 8 August 1942. Under which Viceroy did this occur, and what was Gandhi's famous call to action?
options: Lord Wavell; 'Purna Swaraj'
Lord Linlithgow; 'Do or Die'
Lord Irwin; 'Do or Die'
Lord Linlithgow; 'Simon Go Back'
questions.explanation: The Quit India Movement was launched on 8 August 1942 at the Gowalia Tank Maidan, Bombay, during Lord Linlithgow's tenure as Viceroy. Gandhi's call was 'Do or Die' (Karo ya Maro) — Indians must either free their country or die in the attempt. The Congress passed the Quit India Resolution demanding an end to British rule. Within hours, the British arrested Gandhi, Nehru, and all major Congress leaders. The movement became a leaderless popular uprising — students, workers, farmers rose up across India. The British suppressed it harshly: over 100,000 arrested, military force used, many killed. Linlithgow was the longest-serving Viceroy (1936-1944). 'Purna Swaraj' was the 1929 independence resolution under Irwin; 'Simon Go Back' was the 1928 anti-Simon Commission slogan.
questions.source: Quit India Movement (8 Aug 1942), Linlithgow, 'Do or Die' — tested repeatedly in SSC CGL; also appears as year-matching and slogan-identification questions
